subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 15. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to talk, sing, point and play alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities set out here. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name the seven days of the week in the correct order.
Name the twelve months of the year in the correct order.
Use the words yesterday, today and tomorrow correctly.
Say what day comes before or after a given day.
Say what month comes before or after a given month.
Read o'clock times on a clock face (minute hand on 12).
Read half past times on a clock face (minute hand on 6).
Tell the difference between the hour hand (short) and the minute hand (long).
Put simple daily events in order using time words.

concepts.title: The Days of the Week
concepts.explanation: There are seven days, and they always come in the same order, like a wheel that turns round and round. After Sunday comes Monday again — the week never stops turning. Most of the day names even end in the same sound, ‘day’, which children love to notice. How to teach it: Sing the days as a chant to a tune the child knows, clapping once for each day: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday. Lay out seven stones in a line, one for each day, and touch each stone as you say its name. Tie the…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us sing the days. Clap with me! Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.” You: “Today is Saturday. What day was yesterday?” Child: “Friday!” You: “Yes — Jummah. And…

concepts.title: Yesterday, Today and Tomorrow
concepts.explanation: These three words are how a small child first feels time moving. Today is now, yesterday is the day that has gone, and tomorrow is the day still to come. Children often muddle them, so use them out loud many times a day in real situations.
concepts.example: These three words are how a small child first feels time moving. Today is now, yesterday is the day that has gone, and tomorrow is the day still to come. Children often muddle them, so use them out loud many times a day…

concepts.title: The Months of the Year
concepts.explanation: How to teach it: Chant the months in order, clapping the beat: January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August, September, October, November, December. Use a months wheel or lay out twelve stones in a circle so the child sees that after December we come back round to January. Ask gentle ‘what comes next?’ questions: ‘It is April now — which month comes next?’ Do not expect a young child to know all twelve quickly; going forwards from a given month is a fine first step. 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us…
concepts.example: Notice patterns together, because patterns make memory easy. Two days of the week begin with S (Saturday and Sunday). Two months begin with the letter A (April and August). Spotting these little rhymes and matches turns…

concepts.title: Reading O'Clock Times
concepts.explanation: A clock face has two hands. The short hand is the hour hand and the long hand is the minute hand. When we say a time is ‘o'clock’, it is exactly on the hour: the long hand points straight up to the 12, and the short hand points right at a number. Whatever number the short hand points to is the hour we say. How to teach it: Make the paper-plate clock together. Set the long hand on the 12 and move the short hand to the 3: ‘The long hand is on 12, the short hand is on 3, so it is 3 o'clock.’ Let the child move the…
concepts.example: Tie each time to something the child does. ‘We wake for Fajr. School starts at 8 o'clock. We eat lunch at 12 o'clock. Bedtime is at 8 o'clock at night.’ When a time on the clock means something real, the child cares…

concepts.title: Reading Half Past Times
concepts.explanation: Half past means halfway through the hour — thirty minutes after it. On the clock, the long minute hand points straight down to the 6 (the bottom of the clock), and the short hour hand sits halfway between two numbers. We say ‘half past’ and then the number the hour hand has just gone past. How to teach it: On the paper-plate clock, move the long hand from the 12 all the way down to the 6 and watch how the short hand slides to sit between two numbers. Set half past 2: long hand on 6, short hand between the 2 and…
concepts.example: Connect half past to real moments too: ‘We leave for school at half past 7,’ ‘snack is at half past 3,’ ‘we get ready for bed at half past 8.’ Practise switching between o'clock and half past on the same clock so the…

concepts.title: Putting Events in Order
concepts.explanation: Time is also about sequence — what comes first, next and last. A child who can order the events of their day is building the same ‘what comes next?’ thinking they use for days, months and clock times. Talk through the day in order: ‘First we pray Fajr, then we eat breakfast, then we go to school, then we come home, then we sleep.’ How to teach it: Ask the child to tell you their day back to you in order, using words like first, next, then and last. Mix it up by naming two events and asking which happens first —…
concepts.example: Confusing the two hands. The child reads the minute hand as the hour. Keep repeating ‘short hand = hour, long hand = minutes’ and use hands of different lengths or colours.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

terms: The day it is right now
One of the twelve parts of a year
A time exactly on the hour
The short hand — points to the hour
The long hand — points to the minutes

questions.question: What does “Week” mean?

questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Week means A group of seven days.

questions.question: What does “Yesterday” mean?
options: The day it is right now
One of the twelve parts of a year
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Yesterday means The day before today.

options: The day it is right now
One of the twelve parts of a year

selfAssessment: I can name the seven days of the week in the correct order.
I can name the twelve months of the year in the correct order.
I can use the words yesterday, today and tomorrow correctly.
I can say what day comes before or after a given day.
I can say what month comes before or after a given month.
I can read o'clock times on a clock face (minute hand on 12).
I can read half past times on a clock face (minute hand on 6).
I can tell the difference between the hour hand (short) and the minute hand (long).
